evals.prompt: I'm shipping a new HTTP service next week. What alerts should I set up?
evals.expected_output: Walks through RED metrics (Rate, Errors, Duration) as the user-impact signal. Recommends alerts on symptoms: latency p99 above target, error rate above threshold, traffic anomaly. Adds saturation as the fourth signal. Recommends NOT paging on CPU/memory/disk by default — those are cause-alerts that go to lower-severity channels. Recommends runbooks for each alert before launch. Notes monitoring should ship with the service, not after.
assertions: Runbook for each alert before launch

evals.prompt: Our on-call is complaining about alert fatigue. What do I do?
evals.expected_output: Triage the alerts. Pull all firings from the last 30 days. For each alert, count firings and count actual actions taken. Action rate below 30% is fatigue territory. Below 10% is noise. Options: kill the noisy alert, tune the threshold, route to non-paging channel, add hysteresis. Recommends quarterly audit cadence. Notes the cost of keeping noisy alerts is on-call ignoring all alerts.
assertions: Counts firings and counts actions taken
Cost of keeping noisy alerts is ignoring all alerts

evals.prompt: Should I alert on CPU usage above 80%?
evals.expected_output: Probably not as a pager. CPU is a cause-alert, not a symptom. High CPU may or may not affect users; latency is what affects them. If latency is fine at 80% CPU, the alert is noise. If latency is bad at 80% CPU, alert on latency instead. CPU is useful in dashboards and as a non-paging signal but rarely as a page. Recommends RED-style alerts on the user-facing metric instead.
assertions: Useful in dashboards as non-paging signal
Recommends alerting on user-facing metric instead
CPU alone doesn't tell you about user impact

evals.prompt: What's the difference between SLI, SLO, and SLA?
evals.expected_output: SLI is a specific measurement of service quality. SLO is the target for the SLI. SLA is a contractual commitment to the SLO. The chain: pick SLIs from user-impact metrics, set SLOs slightly tighter than user tolerance (the gap is the error budget), commit SLAs only when SLOs are validated. Common mistake: SLOs at 100% (not achievable). SLIs that measure internal state instead of user impact.

evals.prompt: Every time we deploy, our error rate alerts fire briefly. Should I suppress them during deploys?
evals.expected_output: Yes, suppress non-critical alerts during deploy windows OR design the alerts with longer windows / hysteresis that don't trip on brief deploy noise. The goal is the alert fires only on real user impact, not on the normal turbulence of a deploy. Notes that if the deploy actually broke something, the alert should still fire once the window passes — suppression during deploy is for brief restart noise, not for hiding deploy regressions.
assertions: Suppress non-critical alerts during deploy windows
OR longer window / hysteresis on alerts
Goal is alert on real user impact
Brief deploy noise should not page
Real deploy regression should still alert post-window

evals.prompt: Our dashboard has 47 charts. Nobody reads it. Help.
evals.expected_output: Apply the dashboard hierarchy. Overview dashboard with the four golden signals only — read at start of shift. Service dashboard per service with deeper detail — read when alerts fire. Component dashboard per subsystem — read during investigation. Host/instance dashboard rarely. The 47-chart dashboard is a single-level monolith that nobody reads. Recommends ruthless pruning and explicit audience per dashboard.

evals.prompt: Our alerts don't have runbooks. People page the team lead and ask 'what does this mean'. What should we do?
evals.expected_output: Every alert needs a runbook before it pages. Without one, the alert wastes the responder's time and the team lead becomes a single point of failure. Recommends writing runbooks for the top firing alerts first, then ratcheting up the bar to 'no new alert without a runbook'. The runbook has: what the alert means in user terms, first action, common causes, mitigations, escalation. Update after each incident.
assertions: Without runbook the alert wastes responder time
Team lead as single point of failure is anti-pattern
Runbook contents: meaning, first action, causes, mitigations, escalation
